patterns.name: Scaffolding and Fading
patterns.summary: A more capable person or system temporarily takes over the parts of a task that exceed the learner's current ability. This lets the learner complete the whole task and observe how the hard parts are handled. As the learner internalises the skill, support is systematically withdrawn — faded — until the learner works independently. The method operationalises Vygotsky's Zone of Proximal Development: the gap between what a learner can do alone and what they can do with skilled assistance. Without fading, scaffolding becomes dependency; without calibration, it becomes either over-support or abandonment.
patterns.intent: Enable a learner to accomplish tasks beyond their current unassisted ability by providing calibrated, temporary support that is withdrawn as competence grows.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply when a learner is working at the edge of their current ability. The task should not be so far beyond them that it is incomprehensible, nor so easy that no support is needed. Use at the start of a new skill or problem type where independent performance would produce only failure and frustration. Also use mid-task when a learner stalls: identify precisely which sub-task exceeds them and scaffold that sub-task only. Begin fading as soon as the learner shows reliable, unprompted correct moves. Do not scaffold so heavily that the learner never experiences productive struggle — calibration is continuous, not a one-time setting. Applies equally when a human instructor coaches a person or when an AI agent guides another agent.

inputs.name: Task or problem at the edge of learner ability
inputs.description: A real task that the learner cannot yet complete independently but can complete with targeted support — within the Zone of Proximal Development, not impossibly beyond it.

inputs.name: Diagnostic read of current competence
inputs.description: An accurate assessment of exactly which sub-tasks the learner can handle alone and which exceed current ability, so that scaffolding targets the right gap rather than the whole task.

inputs.name: Graduated support repertoire
inputs.description: A range of support moves available to the instructor or system, from light prompting and redirecting attention through fuller demonstration — matched to the severity of the gap.

outputs.name: More capable learner
outputs.description: A learner who can perform more of the task independently than before — and who has experienced success on the whole task, which builds confidence alongside competence.

outputs.name: Completed task with reduced support
outputs.description: The masterpiece of each scaffolding cycle: a successfully completed task where the learner performed an increasing share of the work, demonstrating genuine internalisation rather than reliance on the scaffold.

steps.name: Diagnose the gap
steps.description: Observe the learner attempting the task and identify precisely which sub-tasks exceed current ability. Avoid global diagnoses — 'they can't do this' — in favour of specific ones: 'they can set up the equation but not select the right algebraic move.'

steps.name: Recruit and frame
steps.description: Engage the learner's interest in completing the task as a whole and make clear what success looks like. Wood, Bruner and Ross called this 'recruitment': the learner must want to reach the goal the scaffold is enabling.

steps.name: Reduce degrees of freedom
steps.description: Simplify or handle the sub-tasks that exceed current ability so the learner can act on the parts they can handle. This is not doing the task for the learner — it is removing the specific blockers so the learner's energy goes to the learnable parts.

steps.name: Mark critical features and maintain direction
steps.description: As the learner works, draw attention to features of the task that matter — errors, decision points, key moves — and keep the learner oriented toward the goal. Avoid overwhelming commentary; target the one or two things that most need attention.

steps.name: Fade support contingently
steps.description: Withdraw each type of support as soon as the learner demonstrates reliable, unprompted correct performance on that sub-task. Fading is not a schedule — it is contingent on observed competence. Remove too slowly and you create dependency; too quickly and you restore the original failure.

principles: Support must be calibrated to the specific gap, not applied globally — over-scaffolding the parts the learner can already do is as harmful as under-scaffolding the parts they cannot.
Fading is not optional: a scaffold that is never removed is not a scaffold but a crutch. The goal of every support move is to make itself unnecessary.
The learner must experience the whole task succeeding, not just the parts they already control — partial completion without the goal gives no model of what competence looks like.
patterns.threshold: The learner can complete the full task independently, without prompting, at the level of quality the scaffold was targeting — and can identify which sub-tasks they needed help with and what they now do differently.
patterns.masterpiece: A task completed entirely without support that the learner could not have completed alone at the outset — demonstrating that the scaffold was a temporary bridge, not a permanent fixture. Ideally accompanied by the learner's own account of which supports were faded and when.

known_uses.name: Reading instruction — Reading Recovery
known_uses.note: Scaffolding and fading is the structural core of Reading Recovery's one-to-one tutoring: the teacher supplies words, corrects miscues, and prompts strategies, withdrawing each support as the child internalises it.

known_uses.name: Pair programming and code review in software apprenticeship
known_uses.note: Senior engineers scaffold junior developers by driving the hard architectural moves during pairing and progressively handing control — fading — as the junior demonstrates understanding.

known_failure_modes.note: Anti-pattern: the instructor provides consistent support indefinitely, never testing whether the learner can proceed without it. The learner completes tasks only when supported, never building independent capability — permanent assistance mistaken for teaching.

Anti-pattern: support is applied to the full task rather than the specific gap, so the learner never gets to exercise the sub-tasks they can already handle. Competence in those areas atrophies and dependency grows across the board.

Anti-pattern: support is withdrawn before the learner has genuinely internalised the sub-task, either on a fixed schedule or because progress seemed fast. The learner is returned to unsupported failure and may lose confidence as well as the skill.

sources.title: The Role of Tutoring in Problem Solving
sources.quote: solve a problem, carry out a task, or achieve a goal which would be beyond his unassisted effort
sources.note: Wood, D. J., Bruner, J. S., & Ross, G. (1976). Journal of Child Psychology and Psychiatry, 17(2), 89–100. p. 90. This is the paper that coined 'scaffolding' in its pedagogical sense and enumerated the six tutoring functions: recruitment, reduction in degrees of freedom, direction maintenance, marking critical features, frustration control, and demonstration.

sources.title: Article Summary: Wood et al. (1976) Role of Tutoring in Problem Solving — Lauren Margulieux
sources.quote: solve a problem, carry out a task, or achieve a goal which would be beyond his unassisted effort (p.90)
sources.note: Academic summary confirming the p. 90 quote and the six scaffolding functions from the original Wood, Bruner & Ross paper.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[Diagnose the gap
Which sub-task exceeds current ability?] --> B[Recruit and frame
Engage learner; show what success looks like]
    B --> C[Reduce degrees of freedom
Handle the blocking sub-tasks; learner acts on the rest]
    C --> D[Mark critical features
Draw attention to errors and key moves]
    D --> E{Learner shows reliable
unsupported performance?}
    E -- No --> C
    E -- Yes --> F[Fade that support
Withdraw contingently]
    F --> G{All supports faded?}
    G -- No --> E
    G -- Yes --> H[Independent task completion
Masterpiece: no scaffold needed]

patterns.example_scenario: A junior data analyst is asked to write a SQL query joining three tables and applying a window function — a task beyond her current skill. Her manager (the scaffolder) sits with her. She can write the basic SELECT and WHERE clauses herself; she stalls at the JOIN logic and has never used a window function. The manager writes the first JOIN for her while narrating the reasoning (reduces degrees of freedom, marks critical features). The analyst writes the second JOIN herself, with the manager catching one alias error (direction maintenance). For the window function, the manager writes it fully and explains each clause (demonstration). Next day the analyst tries a similar query alone; she writes both JOINs correctly. The manager now only prompts on window functions. After three similar tasks, she writes window functions without prompting. The manager fades completely. The analyst's first independent three-table window-function query is the masterpiece.

patterns.name: Worked Examples
patterns.summary: A learner studies a fully worked solution — every step shown, every decision narrated — before attempting to solve similar problems independently. Cognitive load theory explains why this works. Conventional problem-solving forces the learner to manage the search for a solution and acquire the schema for the problem type at the same time. This overloads working memory. A worked example offloads the solution search, freeing cognitive capacity for schema formation. The effect is strongest for novices; once schemas are acquired, problem-solving practice becomes more effective than further examples — the expertise reversal effect. Studied and named by Sweller and Cooper (1985) and grounded in Sweller's cognitive load theory (1988).
patterns.intent: Accelerate schema acquisition in novice learners by replacing the cognitive overhead of unguided problem-solving with the study of fully elaborated solutions.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply at the beginning of a new problem type, domain, or procedure where the learner has no schema to guide problem-solving. In that state, asking them to solve without guidance would produce mostly random search rather than learning. Pair with completion problems (worked example with the final steps omitted) as a transition before full independent practice. Stop using heavily worked examples once the learner has acquired the underlying schema — switch to problem-solving practice to build fluency. Do not use worked examples as the only instructional mode: they must be followed by problems for the learner to attempt, so the schema can be tested and consolidated.

inputs.name: Fully annotated solution
inputs.description: A complete, step-by-step solution to a representative problem, with each step's purpose and decision explained — not just the steps performed but why each was chosen.

inputs.name: Learner with no or minimal schema
inputs.description: A novice or beginner for whom the problem type is genuinely new — the worked-example effect diminishes and can reverse as expertise grows.

inputs.name: Follow-on practice problems
inputs.description: Problems similar to the worked example for the learner to attempt after study, allowing the newly forming schema to be applied and tested.

outputs.name: More capable learner
outputs.description: A learner who has formed a problem-solving schema for the example type and can apply it to similar problems with substantially fewer errors and in less time than a learner who only practised problem-solving from the start.

outputs.name: Acquired schema
outputs.description: The masterpiece: an internalised problem type template — a mental structure that allows the learner to recognise future instances of the problem class and know which moves to apply, without re-deriving the solution from scratch.

steps.name: Select a representative worked example
steps.description: Choose a problem that is prototypical for the target problem class — complex enough to show all the important moves, simple enough to be comprehensible in one pass. Avoid edge cases as first examples.

steps.name: Annotate every step with its rationale
steps.description: For each step, make explicit: what was done, why this move rather than an alternative, and what constraint or principle dictates this choice. The annotation is the teaching — a list of steps without explanation is a recipe, not a worked example.

steps.name: Have the learner study the example actively
steps.description: Ask the learner to read through the full solution and then to explain each step back without looking — self-explanation — before attempting practice problems. Passive reading without self-explanation substantially reduces the learning gain.

steps.name: Transition to completion problems
steps.description: Provide problems where the first steps are worked and the final steps are blank for the learner to complete. This bridges study and full independent practice, reducing the jump in cognitive demand.

steps.name: Move to full independent practice
steps.description: Once the learner can complete similar problems with few errors, switch entirely to problem-solving practice. Continued heavy reliance on worked examples beyond this point produces the expertise reversal effect: the worked-out steps become interference rather than support.

principles: Working memory is the bottleneck: any instructional design that demands simultaneous schema search and solution search will overload it for novices. Worked examples solve only one problem at a time — schema acquisition — by removing the other.

Self-explanation is not optional: the learning gain from a worked example comes largely from the learner constructing their own account of why each step was taken. An example studied passively yields much weaker schema acquisition.

The effect reverses with expertise: for learners who have already acquired the schema, worked examples become redundant and cognitively intrusive — problem-solving practice is then the superior method.
patterns.threshold: The learner can, given a new problem of the same type, identify the problem class, recall the sequence of moves, and apply them without consulting the worked example — and can explain why each step was taken.
patterns.masterpiece: A novel problem from the same class solved correctly and efficiently on the first attempt, with the learner able to articulate the schema they applied — demonstrating that they have acquired the pattern, not just memorised the specific example.

known_uses.name: Mathematics and physics instruction
known_uses.note: Textbooks in STEM routinely lead chapters with worked examples before practice problems; cognitive load research has formalised and optimised this long-standing practice.

known_uses.name: Programming tutorials and coding bootcamps
known_uses.note: Step-through code walkthroughs — narrated, annotated — function as worked examples; learners then attempt similar exercises. The pattern is endemic to code education precisely because it manages the cognitive load of a new syntax and a new problem simultaneously.

known_failure_modes.note: Anti-pattern: the learner reads the worked example without self-explaining each step, then proceeds directly to practice problems. Passive reading produces weak schema acquisition; the learner can follow the example line by line but cannot reconstruct the reasoning on a new problem.

Anti-pattern: providing fully worked examples to learners who have already acquired the schema. The expertise reversal effect means that for these learners, the redundant worked steps become cognitive interference — problem-solving practice would produce faster and deeper learning.

Anti-pattern: presenting worked examples as the complete instructional sequence, with no follow-on problems for the learner to attempt. The schema formed from study alone remains untested and fragile — it cannot be generalised without application.

sources.title: The Use of Worked Examples as a Substitute for Problem Solving in Learning Algebra
sources.quote: The Use of Worked Examples as a Substitute for Problem Solving in Learning Algebra
sources.note: Sweller, J., & Cooper, G. A. (1985). Cognition and Instruction, 2(1), 59–89. The founding experimental paper demonstrating that algebra students who studied worked examples performed roughly twice as fast and made ~80% fewer errors on similar post-test problems compared to those who only practised problem-solving. Named the worked-example effect.

sources.title: Cognitive Load During Problem Solving: Effects on Learning
sources.quote: conventional problem solving in the form of means-ends analysis requires a relatively large amount of cognitive processing capacity which is consequently unavailable for schema acquisition
sources.note: Sweller, J. (1988). Cognitive Science, 12(2), 257–285. Established cognitive load theory as the theoretical foundation for the worked-example effect, showing that means-ends problem solving consumes working memory capacity that would otherwise be available for schema formation.

sources.title: Worked-example effect — Wikipedia
sources.quote: The worked example effect is the best known and most widely studied of the cognitive load effects
sources.note: Sweller (2006) cited in Wikipedia article on the worked-example effect. Confirms the effect's canonical status within cognitive load theory.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[Select representative worked example
Prototypical problem for target class] --> B[Annotate every step with rationale
What + why for each move]
    B --> C[Learner studies example actively
Self-explanation: reconstruct reasoning]
    C --> D[Completion problems
First steps worked; final steps blank]
    D --> E{Schema acquired?
Learner solves similar with few errors}
    E -- No --> C
    E -- Yes --> F[Switch to full problem-solving practice
Expertise reversal: examples now interfere]
    F --> G[Masterpiece: novel problem solved
from schema, no example needed]

patterns.example_scenario: A bootcamp instructor is teaching learners to parse and transform JSON with Python. Instead of assigning exercises immediately, she opens with a single worked example: a real API response, a target output format, and a complete Python script that achieves the transformation. She annotates each section in comments: why she chose a list comprehension rather than a for-loop, why she handles the missing-key case with .get() rather than direct indexing, and why she separates parsing from transformation into two functions. She asks each learner to read the example, close it, and then explain the purpose of each annotated section aloud to a partner before touching a keyboard. After that self-explanation pass, she gives a completion problem: the first function is provided, the second is blank. Only after learners complete both halves independently does she assign a fully novel transformation problem. Learners who self-explained the worked example make substantially fewer off-by-one and key-error mistakes than a parallel cohort that went straight to exercises.

patterns.name: Formative Assessment Checkpoints
patterns.summary: Formative assessment is a continuous, embedded practice of gathering evidence about learner understanding and adjusting instruction — or the learner's own effort — in response to that evidence. Unlike summative assessment, which judges what was learned after the fact, formative assessment intervenes while learning is still in progress. Black and Wiliam's landmark 1998 review found strong evidence that improving formative assessment raises achievement standards, and that the feedback loop must close in both directions: the teacher adjusting instruction and the learner adjusting their own approach through self-assessment. Checkpoints make the practice visible and regular rather than implicit and occasional.
patterns.intent: Keep learning on track by regularly surfacing the gap between current understanding and the target, then using that gap information to adjust instruction or learner effort before it becomes a terminal failure.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply throughout any learning sequence — not just at the end. Insert checkpoints wherever the learning sequence branches or depends on a prior concept being solid: if the prior concept is shaky, subsequent instruction builds on sand. Use brief, low-stakes checks — exit tickets, targeted questions, short tasks — rather than full tests, so the checkpoint itself does not consume disproportionate time. Apply especially when the instructor suspects misconceptions have formed, when learner engagement has dropped, or when the pace of instruction has been faster than normal. Train learners in self-assessment as early as possible so they can initiate their own checkpoints without waiting for external prompting.

inputs.name: Learning objective in checkable form
inputs.description: A specific, observable outcome that can be assessed in a short task or question — not 'understands recursion' but 'can write a recursive function that correctly handles the base case.'

inputs.name: Low-stakes checkpoint mechanism
inputs.description: A quick probe of understanding — question, brief task, exit ticket, peer explanation, or diagnostic quiz — that generates evidence without the stress and time cost of formal evaluation.

inputs.name: Commitment to act on the evidence
inputs.description: A willingness from the instructor or learner to actually change what happens next based on checkpoint results, rather than treating the checkpoint as a box-ticking exercise.

outputs.name: More capable learner
outputs.description: A learner whose misconceptions were caught and corrected while the material was still current, rather than discovered retrospectively in a final assessment when remediation is harder and more costly.

outputs.name: Adjusted instructional path
outputs.description: The masterpiece of a checkpoint cycle: a concrete change to what happens next — reteaching a concept, slowing pace, giving targeted feedback, redirecting a learner's self-study — that demonstrably closes the gap identified.

steps.name: Specify the checkpoint objective
steps.description: Before the checkpoint, articulate exactly what evidence of understanding you need: which concept, which operation, which misconception you are probing. Vague objectives produce checkpoints that generate noise rather than signal.

steps.name: Design a minimal, targeted probe
steps.description: Create the smallest checkpoint that generates reliable evidence of the specific objective. A single well-chosen question often outperforms a ten-item quiz in revealing whether a concept is understood or misunderstood.

steps.name: Administer and collect evidence
steps.description: Run the checkpoint in a low-stakes mode — no grades, no judgment — so learners respond honestly. Anonymous or group responses (show-of-hands, shared whiteboards, polling tools) reduce social pressure that masks real understanding.

steps.name: Interpret the evidence and decide
steps.description: Categorise responses: who understands, who has a specific misconception, who is guessing. Make a concrete decision about what changes: reteach, pair stronger with weaker, send one group ahead, slow down. The checkpoint is useless without this decision.

steps.name: Act and close the loop with the learner
steps.description: Implement the change. Crucially, give learners feedback on their checkpoint response — not a grade but a specific note on what was correct, what was wrong, and what to do next. Black and Wiliam are explicit: feedback must tell the learner how to improve, not just whether they succeeded.

principles: Formative assessment works only when the loop closes: evidence gathered but not acted upon is surveillance, not assessment. The loop must close for both instructor (adjusting teaching) and learner (adjusting effort and strategy).

Feedback should be task-referenced, not person-referenced: telling a learner their answer was wrong is far less useful than telling them exactly which step went wrong and what to do instead. Grade-based or comparative feedback provokes ego responses that block learning.

Learner self-assessment is not a nice-to-have: training learners to assess their own understanding is what makes formative assessment scalable and persistent. A learner who can identify their own gap does not need an instructor at every checkpoint.
patterns.threshold: The learner can accurately self-assess their own understanding of the current topic — identifying what they know confidently, what they know partially, and what they do not know — and can initiate a request for targeted feedback on the specific gap.
patterns.masterpiece: A learning sequence in which every checkpoint generated a concrete instructional adjustment, the learner's self-assessments were consistently accurate against external evidence, and the final summative assessment showed substantially higher performance than a comparable sequence without checkpoints.

known_uses.name: Black Box reform in UK schools
known_uses.org: King's College London / UK school reform
known_uses.note: Black and Wiliam's 1998 review directly prompted large-scale formative assessment reforms in UK schools, influencing national assessment policy and teacher professional development for over a decade.

known_uses.name: Automated progress checks in e-learning platforms
known_uses.note: Adaptive learning platforms embed formative checkpoints after every concept module — quiz questions, exercise sets, mastery gates — and adjust the learner's path based on performance, operationalising Black and Wiliam's loop at scale.

known_failure_modes.note: Anti-pattern: checkpoints are run on schedule but instruction does not change based on results. The checkpoint becomes a ritual with no feedback loop — learners sense the disconnect and stop engaging honestly with the probe.

Anti-pattern: the only feedback returned to the learner is a score or grade. Black and Wiliam's research is explicit that grades without task-specific guidance on improvement do not improve learning and can actively harm motivation.

Anti-pattern: so many checkpoints are inserted that assessment time crowds out learning time, and learners experience checkpoint fatigue. Formative checkpoints must be minimal — their purpose is to generate a decision signal, not to be a comprehensive measure.

patterns.ecosystem: education, professional development, e-learning, autonomous-agent training

sources.title: Inside the Black Box: Raising Standards Through Classroom Assessment
sources.quote: There is a body of firm evidence that formative assessment is an essential component of classroom work and that its development can raise standards of achievement. We know of no other way of raising standards for which such a strong prima facie case can be made.
sources.note: Black, P., & Wiliam, D. (1998). Phi Delta Kappan, 80(2), 139–144. The landmark review of 250+ studies on formative assessment. Established the evidence base and defined the core feedback-loop model.

sources.title: Inside the Black Box: Classic Education Gold from Wiliam and Black — Teacher Head
sources.quote: Feedback to any pupil should be about the particular qualities of his or her work, with advice on what he or she can do to improve, and should avoid comparisons with other pupils.
sources.note: Quotes from Black & Wiliam 1998 reproduced in a practitioner review, confirming the task-specific, non-comparative feedback principle.

sources.title: Revisiting Inside the Black Box 20 Years On — Corwin Connect
sources.quote: For formative assessment to be productive, pupils should be trained in self-assessment so that they can understand the main purposes of their learning and thereby grasp what they need to do to achieve.
sources.note: Black & Wiliam 1998 quote on learner self-assessment, reproduced in the 20th anniversary retrospective. Confirms the self-assessment principle as original to the 1998 paper.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[Specify checkpoint objective
What evidence of understanding do I need?] --> B[Design minimal targeted probe
Smallest check that generates reliable signal]
    B --> C[Administer low-stakes
Anonymous or group mode; no grades]
    C --> D[Interpret evidence
Who understands? Who has which misconception?]
    D --> E{Gap found?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Act: reteach / target feedback / slow down]
    F --> G[Close loop with learner
Task-specific guidance on what to do next]
    G --> A
    E -- No --> H[Advance to next objective]
    H --> A

patterns.example_scenario: A software engineering instructor finishes explaining database indexing and suspects learners have conflated 'an index speeds every query' with 'an index speeds queries on the indexed column'. Rather than waiting for the end-of-week assignment, she runs a two-minute checkpoint: learners vote anonymously on a poll — 'Which of these three queries will the index help?' — with one correct answer and two plausible distractors. Results show 60% chose a distractor — a query on a non-indexed column. Instead of moving to the next topic, she reteaches the single concept of column-specificity, works one counterexample, then re-polls. Results shift to 85% correct. She also returns written feedback to the 15% still wrong, telling them their answer assumed the index covers all columns and asking them to check which column the WHERE clause uses. Three learners who self-assessed as confident before the checkpoint now flag the concept as shaky. The checkpoint trained their self-assessment accuracy alongside the content.

patterns.name: Flipped Classroom
patterns.summary: Instruction is moved out of the classroom into asynchronous pre-work — typically video — and the time formerly spent on instruction is used for active application, problem-solving, and teacher-guided interaction. 'That which is traditionally done in class is now done at home, and that which is traditionally done as homework is now completed in class' (Bergmann & Sams, 2012, p. 13). The logic is that passive reception of new material is the part of learning that least requires a trained instructor present; active struggle with application is precisely where expert guidance is most valuable. Popularised by Colorado chemistry teachers Jonathan Bergmann and Aaron Sams from 2007, who began recording video lessons so absent students could catch up — and found the results were better for everyone.
patterns.intent: Reallocate classroom time from passive information delivery to active, instructor-supported application, so that expert guidance is available at the moment of greatest cognitive need.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply when learners can reliably access pre-work outside class and when the instructor's most distinctive contribution is not delivering information but responding to learner difficulty in real time. Use when class time is scarce and is currently dominated by lecture — any reallocation toward active work with expert feedback will improve learning. Do not use when learners lack access to video or internet outside class, when pre-work compliance is very low, or when the subject matter has no clear application component that benefits from peer and instructor interaction. Also avoid when the instructor's live explanation is genuinely irreplaceable and hard to pre-record effectively.

inputs.name: Asynchronous instructional content
inputs.description: Video lectures, annotated readings, or other self-paced material that delivers the foundational concepts learners need before attending the active session. Must be accessible outside class and navigable at the learner's own pace.

inputs.name: Accountability mechanism for pre-work
inputs.description: A short quiz, question prompt, or entry ticket that confirms learners engaged with the pre-work before the active session — without which a large proportion will arrive unprepared and active time collapses.

inputs.name: Active in-class tasks
inputs.description: Problem sets, projects, case studies, or peer-teaching activities that require and develop understanding of the pre-work material and cannot be completed without it — the tasks that make the reclaimed class time worthwhile.

outputs.name: More capable learner
outputs.description: A learner who has encountered concepts before class, applied them with instructor and peer support during class, and received targeted feedback at the moment of greatest confusion — rather than discovering confusion alone at home during homework.

outputs.name: Instructor time spent on high-value interaction
outputs.description: The masterpiece at the instructor level: class time in which the instructor spends no time on information delivery and all available time on individual and small-group diagnosis, feedback, and targeted reteaching.

steps.name: Audit current class time
steps.description: Map what actually happens during class: how many minutes go to lecture, how many to active work, how many to Q&A. Identify which parts of the lecture can be pre-recorded without loss and which genuinely benefit from live presence.

steps.name: Create or curate pre-work content
steps.description: Record short, focused videos (10–15 minutes is the effective ceiling for a single concept) or curate existing materials. Annotate key moments. Structure so the learner can pause, rewind, and revisit at their own pace.

steps.name: Design a pre-work accountability check
steps.description: Create a brief quiz or question prompt that learners complete before arriving, confirming engagement with the content. Make it low-stakes but required — it is a readiness gate, not a performance measure.

steps.name: Design active in-class tasks
steps.description: Replace lecture time with tasks that require application of the pre-work: problems, case analyses, peer instruction, design challenges. Ensure tasks are genuinely difficult enough to need instructor support so learners experience the value of the reclaimed time.

steps.name: Use class time for guided active work
steps.description: Circulate continuously during active sessions. Spend time with struggling learners; extend successful ones. The instructor's role shifts from presenter to expert coach — available at the moment of greatest difficulty.

principles: Class time is the scarcest resource and the only time expert feedback is reliably available: spend it on the tasks that most need that feedback, not on content delivery the learner can receive alone.

Pre-work only works if it is done: accountability mechanisms are not optional add-ons but structural components of the flip. Without them, the active class session degenerates into re-teaching the pre-work to the unprepared majority.
patterns.threshold: The learner arrives at the active session with sufficient foundational understanding to engage productively with the application tasks without needing the instructor to re-deliver the pre-work material.
patterns.masterpiece: A session in which the instructor does not deliver a single minute of whole-class lecture, spends the entire period responding to individual and small-group difficulty, and learners complete application tasks at a depth they could not have reached working alone.

known_uses.name: Bergmann and Sams — Woodland Park High School chemistry
known_uses.note: Bergmann and Sams began recording video lessons in 2007 to support absent students; they found in-class time reclaimed for active work improved outcomes for all students and popularised the approach through their 2012 book.

known_uses.name: Khan Academy and classroom partnerships
known_uses.note: Khan Academy's video library was explicitly adopted by teachers as the pre-work component of flipped classrooms, with class time reserved for working through problems — a large-scale instantiation of the Bergmann-Sams model.

known_failure_modes.note: Anti-pattern: learners do not engage with pre-work before the session, so the active class time is spent re-delivering the foundational content and the flip produces no gain over a traditional lecture. Occurs when pre-work accountability mechanisms are absent or non-enforced.

Anti-pattern: the instructor reclaims class time from lecture but fills it with more passive activity — watching additional videos in class, whole-class Q&A without individual work — rather than genuinely active application. The flip moves the location of passive learning without changing its nature.

Anti-pattern: the model is applied in a context where a subset of learners lacks reliable access to video or internet outside class, so the flip systematically disadvantages those learners who most need support. Equity must be assessed before adopting the model.

patterns.ecosystem: education, corporate training, online and blended learning

sources.title: Flip Your Classroom: Reach Every Student in Every Class Every Day
sources.quote: that which is traditionally done in class is now done at home, and that which is traditionally done as homework is now completed in class
sources.note: Bergmann, J., & Sams, A. (2012). ISTE / ASCD. p. 13. The defining statement of the model by its originators.

sources.title: The Flipped Classroom — Education Next
sources.quote: I now have time to work individually with students. I talk to every student in every classroom every day
sources.note: Bergmann quoted in Education Next on the core pedagogical gain of the flipped model: instructor time freed for individual interaction.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[Learner watches pre-work video at home
Self-paced; pause and rewind] --> B[Pre-work accountability check
Quiz or entry ticket before class]
    B --> C{Pre-work done?}
    C -- No --> D[Targeted catch-up or pre-work reassignment]
    D --> C
    C -- Yes --> E[Active in-class work
Problems, cases, projects]
    E --> F[Instructor circulates
Coaches struggling learners; extends strong ones]
    F --> G[Formative checkpoint
Is understanding solid enough to continue?]
    G -- No --> F
    G -- Yes --> H[Masterpiece: application task completed
with expert feedback at point of difficulty]

patterns.example_scenario: A corporate data science team lead runs a weekly Friday workshop. Previously each session started with 30 minutes of lecture on a new technique, then 20 minutes of exercise, then 10 minutes of questions. She flips the model: on Thursday she releases a 12-minute narrated walkthrough video of the week's technique with a five-question quiz due by Friday 8am. Friday's 60 minutes are now entirely active: the first 10 minutes review the quiz results as a group, the remaining 50 minutes are spent on a case study problem the team can only solve by applying the technique. She circulates and targets the two engineers whose quiz answers show conceptual gaps. By the end of the session every engineer has worked through the full case with real data and received at least one targeted comment from the team lead. The following week's quiz shows retention substantially higher than in the old lecture format.

patterns.name: 70-20-10 Model
patterns.summary: A heuristic for structuring professional development across three learning modes: roughly 70% from on-the-job experience (challenging assignments, real problems), 20% from social learning (feedback, coaching, observing role models), and 10% from formal training (courses, reading). Originated from a Center for Creative Leadership study of successful managers in the 1980s, codified by Lombardo and Eichinger in their 1996 Career Architect Development Planner. The model challenges the common organisational inversion — where formal training dominates L&D budgets while on-the-job experience is left to chance — and argues that development is primarily driven by experience, mediated by social reflection, and supplemented by structured content. The percentages are indicative, not empirically prescriptive: no randomised study has validated 70/20/10 as a precise ratio. It is best used as a design lens, not a formula.
patterns.intent: Design professional development that allocates most learning opportunity to challenging real work, uses social feedback and coaching to extract learning from that work, and positions formal content as a targeted supplement rather than the main event.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply when designing or auditing a professional development programme, career development plan, or organisational learning strategy. Use when formal training is consuming disproportionate budget and time relative to its actual contribution to skill development. Use with individual contributors and managers who can be intentionally placed in stretch assignments — roles with genuine challenge that exceed current capability. Do not use as a rigid formula to allocate learning hours: the ratios are a provocative framing, not a measurement target. Do not use where the 70% (real work) is unavailable or unsafe — where mistakes have irreversible consequences, formal training and simulation must carry more weight.

inputs.name: Stretch assignments and real challenges
inputs.description: Job responsibilities or projects that push the learner beyond current comfort, requiring new skills and decisions. The 70% only works if the work is genuinely challenging — routine work produces routine skill, not development.

inputs.name: Feedback and coaching relationships
inputs.description: Managers, mentors, or peers who provide regular, specific feedback on how the learner is performing in the stretch role and who model effective practice through their own visible behaviour.

inputs.name: Targeted formal content
inputs.description: Courses, books, workshops, or structured programmes that give the learner frameworks and language for what they are experiencing — most effective when timed to moments of active challenge in the 70%, not delivered speculatively.

outputs.name: More capable learner
outputs.description: A professional whose development was driven by real work rather than by training alone, and who can therefore apply their capability in ambiguous real-world situations — not just in the conditions of the training room.

outputs.name: Intentional development plan
outputs.description: The masterpiece: a written development plan in which the majority of learning activity is structured around specific stretch assignments, supported by named coaching relationships, with targeted formal inputs scheduled at moments of active challenge — and in which the learner can articulate what they are learning and from which mode.

steps.name: Audit current development allocation
steps.description: Estimate how the learner's development time and budget is currently split across experience, social learning, and formal training. Most organisations find formal training is over-weighted relative to its contribution to actual capability change.

steps.name: Identify stretch assignment for the 70%
steps.description: Find or create a real work assignment that is meaningfully beyond the learner's current capability — not a token challenge but a role, project, or responsibility with real stakes and real difficulty. Document what skills this assignment is expected to develop.

steps.name: Structure the 20%: coaching and feedback
steps.description: Arrange regular feedback from a manager, mentor, or peer — not annual performance reviews but frequent, specific observations on how the learner is performing in the stretch role. Where possible, structure exposure to role models so the learner can observe effective practice directly.

steps.name: Select targeted formal inputs for the 10%
steps.description: Identify the specific concepts, frameworks, or skills the learner needs to make sense of what they are experiencing in the 70%. Time formal inputs to moments of active challenge — not as orientation before the challenge begins.

steps.name: Review and adapt the plan
steps.description: Revisit the development plan regularly — monthly or quarterly — to check whether the 70% challenge is still genuinely stretching, whether coaching is delivering useful feedback, and whether formal content is being applied. Adjust proportions if the assignment has become routine.

principles: Experience is the primary driver of development: formal training that is not anchored to real challenge does not transfer. Most of what professionals need to learn cannot be learned in a classroom.

The 20% mediates the 70%: raw experience without structured reflection and feedback does not automatically produce learning — it can produce reinforcement of bad habits as easily as development of good ones. Coaching and social feedback extract the learning from the experience.

The 10% must be timed: formal content delivered long before or long after the relevant stretch experience has low transfer. Delivered during active challenge, it gives the learner language and frameworks for what they are living through.
patterns.threshold: The learner can, without prompting, identify the stretch assignment they are currently developing through, name the feedback relationships that are shaping their learning, and cite the specific formal inputs that gave them frameworks for what they are experiencing.
patterns.masterpiece: A completed development plan with documented evidence across all three modes: the stretch assignment and what it demanded, the coaching moments that shaped reflection, and the formal inputs that provided frameworks — culminating in a capability the learner can demonstrate in a real-world context that did not exist in the training room.

known_uses.name: CCL leadership development programmes
known_uses.note: The model originated from CCL research and is embedded in CCL's own leadership development methodology; it has been used as a planning and auditing framework for executive development programmes worldwide.

known_uses.name: Corporate L&D strategy design
known_uses.org: Various; widely adopted in enterprise L&D
known_uses.note: The 70-20-10 framework is widely used in corporate L&D as a budget and programme design heuristic, prompting organisations to examine whether their formal training spend matches the model's prediction of where development actually occurs.

known_failure_modes.note: Anti-pattern: treating the 70-20-10 ratios as empirically validated targets and attempting to measure or report actual hours against them. The original research did not prescribe exact percentages, and the numbers were never validated as a causal formula; designing to hit the numbers is cargo-cult L&D.

Anti-pattern: relying on stretch assignments alone — the 70% — without the 20% coaching and feedback that extracts learning from experience. Raw experience without structured reflection can entrench bad habits as effectively as it builds good ones.

Anti-pattern: the development plan consists entirely of courses and certifications — the 10% — with no identified stretch assignment and no coaching relationships. Organisations frequently default to this because formal training is easier to schedule and measure than real developmental experiences.

patterns.ecosystem: corporate learning and development, leadership development, professional education

sources.title: The Career Architect Development Planner
sources.quote: The odds are that development will be about 70% from on-the-job experiences - working on tasks and problems; about 20% from feedback and working around good and bad examples of the need; and 10% from courses and reading.
sources.note: Lombardo, M. M., & Eichinger, R. W. (1996). Lominger, Minneapolis. 1st edition. Original source of the 70-20-10 proportions, based on CCL research by McCall, Lombardo, and Eichinger in the 1980s. Note: the phrase '70:20:10' does not appear in the original book; it is a later shorthand for the proportions described.

sources.title: 70/20/10 model (learning and development) — Wikipedia
sources.quote: Development generally begins with a realization of current or future need and the motivation to do something about it. This might come from feedback, a mistake, watching other people's reactions, failing or not being up to a task – in other words, from experience.
sources.note: Wikipedia article on the model, citing the original Lombardo & Eichinger 1996 source and confirming the CCL research background.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[Audit current development allocation
Where does learning actually happen?] --> B[Identify stretch assignment — 70%
Real challenge beyond current capability]
    B --> C[Structure coaching and feedback — 20%
Frequent specific observation from mentor or manager]
    C --> D[Select targeted formal inputs — 10%
Timed to active challenge, not scheduled speculatively]
    D --> E[Learner works in stretch role
Experience is the primary driver]
    E --> F[Coaching extracts learning from experience
Reflection and feedback on what happened]
    F --> G[Formal content gives frameworks
for what is being lived through]
    G --> H{Assignment still stretching?}
    H -- No: routine now --> B
    H -- Yes --> E
    H -- Complete --> I[Masterpiece: demonstrated capability
in real-world context]

patterns.example_scenario: A manager at a mid-size software company wants to develop a senior engineer into an engineering manager. The L&D team had planned to enrol the engineer in a three-day management workshop. Instead they apply the 70-20-10 lens. The 70%: the engineer is given responsibility for running a small cross-functional delivery team for six months — real accountability, real stakeholders, real deadlines. The 20%: the manager meets fortnightly with the engineer specifically to review what happened in the team that week — what decisions were made, what conflicts arose, what the engineer would do differently. The CTO is visible in a monthly all-hands and the engineer is explicitly invited to observe how the CTO handles difficult conversations. The 10%: the management workshop is rescheduled to month two of the assignment, once the engineer has encountered real management challenges and has questions the workshop can actually answer. At the end of six months the engineer can demonstrate, through the team's outcomes and their own retrospective, capabilities that no workshop could have produced.

patterns.name: Spiral Curriculum
patterns.summary: Core ideas are introduced at a simple, intuitive level and then revisited repeatedly at increasing levels of complexity, formalism, and connection to other knowledge. Each revisit builds on what was internalised before, going deeper and broader rather than introducing unrelated new content. The approach is grounded in Jerome Bruner's 1960 claim that 'any subject can be taught in some intellectually honest form to any child at any stage of development' (The Process of Education, p. 30) — the curriculum is not locked behind prerequisites but engineered so that early encounters are already genuine rather than dumbed-down. The spiral is the alternative to two common failure modes: covering content once and never returning to it (breadth without depth), or withholding a topic until all prerequisites are complete (sequentialism that excludes early learners from the most important ideas).
patterns.intent: Build deep, connected understanding by introducing core ideas early in accessible form, then returning to them at progressively higher levels of complexity and abstraction throughout the learning sequence.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply when designing curricula, courses, or learning sequences for topics with genuine conceptual depth that cannot be fully treated in a single encounter. Use when the subject has foundational ideas that underpin everything else — so early, intuitive exposure accelerates later formal treatment. Use when you have access to the learner over a long enough time horizon to revisit: a single lesson or a short workshop cannot spiral. Also apply when learners are diverse in current ability — the spiral naturally creates multiple entry points for the same idea at different levels. Do not use when the learning objective is a single, flat procedure with no underlying structure to revisit: spiralling a checklist produces redundancy, not depth.

inputs.name: Core ideas worth revisiting
inputs.description: The foundational concepts, principles, or patterns of the domain — the ideas that everything else connects to. Not all content is worth spiralling; only the ideas that become richer and more connected with each revisit.

inputs.name: Multi-stage curriculum or learning sequence
inputs.description: Enough time and contact with the learner to plan at least two revisits of each core idea at genuinely different levels of treatment — the spiral cannot be completed in a single session.

inputs.name: Level-appropriate representations
inputs.description: For each core idea: concrete, intuitive treatments for early encounters; progressively more formal, abstract, and connected treatments for later revisits. Each representation must be intellectually honest — a simplification, not a falsehood.

outputs.name: More capable learner
outputs.description: A learner for whom core ideas are not isolated facts memorised once but living, revisited, deepening structures — who can connect current formal understanding to early intuitive encounters and who finds later complexity less daunting because the terrain is familiar.

outputs.name: Integrated, deepened understanding
outputs.description: The masterpiece: a learner who, at the end of the sequence, can explain a core idea at every level of the spiral — from the initial intuitive encounter through the most formal treatment — and can articulate how each revisit added a dimension that the previous level could not capture.

steps.name: Identify the core ideas
steps.description: Map the domain and identify the five to ten ideas that underpin everything else — the ideas that experts return to constantly, that connect across topics, and that become richer with deeper study. These are the candidates for spiralling.

steps.name: Design the first encounter: intuitive and honest
steps.description: Plan the earliest treatment of each core idea — concrete, accessible, using familiar context — but intellectually honest. The learner should encounter the real idea, not a cartoon of it. Bruner's standard: the form must be honest at every stage, even if simplified.

steps.name: Map the revisit sequence
steps.description: Plan when and how each core idea will be revisited across the curriculum. Each revisit should add a new dimension: greater formalism, connection to another concept, a more demanding context, or a more rigorous proof. Document what each revisit adds that the previous encounter could not.

steps.name: Connect each revisit explicitly to prior encounters
steps.description: At each revisit, explicitly reference the earlier encounter, telling the learner that they first saw this as X and that now it can be said more precisely as Y. This connection is what makes the spiral a scaffold rather than a fresh start; it honours and builds on prior knowledge.

steps.name: Check that each level is genuinely new
steps.description: Audit each revisit: does this encounter actually deepen understanding, or does it just repeat the previous level with different examples? If the learner cannot say what is new about this revisit, it is not a spiral move — it is re-teaching.

principles: Every level of the spiral must be intellectually honest: early simplifications must be genuine starting points, not misconceptions that later teaching has to undo. A curriculum that introduces a wrong model early to make it easy creates remediation work later that the spiral format was meant to avoid.

Revisits must be connected explicitly to prior encounters: without that explicit connection, a spiral becomes a series of disconnected introductions to the same topic, not a cumulative deepening. The learner must recognise what they already know and see what is being added.

Not everything should be spiralled: choose the ideas worth revisiting — the generative core of the domain — and cover more peripheral content once. Spiralling everything produces a curriculum where everything feels equally important and nothing deepens.
patterns.threshold: The learner can, for a core idea they have revisited at multiple levels, explain the earliest intuitive encounter and each subsequent level — articulating what each revisit added and why the simpler version was a genuine starting point rather than a mistake.
patterns.masterpiece: A completed curriculum sequence in which the learner, asked about a core idea at the end of the programme, can reconstruct the full spiral — from first intuitive encounter through the most formal treatment — connecting each level to the next and explaining why the idea had to be revisited rather than fully taught in a single pass.

known_uses.name: Mathematics curricula — NCTM standards
known_uses.org: National Council of Teachers of Mathematics
known_uses.note: US K-12 mathematics curriculum is explicitly organised on spiral principles: fractions, for example, are introduced in concrete form in early grades, revisited as rational numbers in middle school, and treated formally in algebra and beyond.

known_uses.name: Medical education — basic science to clinical revisit
known_uses.note: Pathophysiology is introduced in pre-clinical years in simplified form, revisited in clinical rotations in diagnostic context, and revisited again in subspecialty training — a classic spiral across the entire medical curriculum.

known_failure_modes.note: Anti-pattern: a topic is revisited but at the same level of complexity as before — new examples, same concepts. The learner does not experience any deepening; the spiral becomes a loop. Each revisit must add a genuinely new dimension or it is re-teaching disguised as spiral.

Anti-pattern: the first encounter presents a model that is not a simplification but an error — a false rule that has to be actively un-taught at the next revisit. For example, teaching that subtraction 'always gives a smaller number' requires costly remediation when negative numbers arrive. Bruner's standard of intellectual honesty at every level is not optional.

Anti-pattern: the curriculum designer spirals all content equally, including peripheral detail that does not merit revisiting. The result is a curriculum with no clear hierarchy — learners cannot identify what is central and what is peripheral — and revisits consume time that could have gone to depth on core ideas.

sources.title: The Process of Education
sources.quote: We begin with the hypothesis that any subject can be taught in some intellectually honest form to any child at any stage of development
sources.note: Bruner, J. S. (1960). Harvard University Press. p. 30. The founding statement of the spiral curriculum hypothesis. Bruner's requirement of 'intellectual honesty' at every stage — not just any simplification, but a genuine representation — is the design constraint that distinguishes the spiral from mere dumbing-down.

sources.title: Reconfiguring Bruner: Compressing the Spiral Curriculum — Kappan Online
sources.quote: We begin with the hypothesis that any subject can be taught in some intellectually honest form to any child at any stage of development (1960, p. 30).
sources.note: Kappan article citing Bruner p. 30, confirming the verbatim quote and discussing how the spiral curriculum operates in practice.

sources.title: Spiral Curriculum — EBSCO Research Starters
sources.quote: Bruner (1960) said revisiting topics deepens learner understanding with added layers.
sources.note: EBSCO Research Starters entry on the spiral curriculum, confirming Bruner (1960) as the canonical source and the revisiting-with-deepening principle.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[Identify core ideas worth revisiting
Foundational concepts that underpin the domain] --> B[First encounter: intuitive and honest
Concrete, accessible — but intellectually genuine]
    B --> C[Learner internalises first level]
    C --> D[Revisit 1: add formalism or new context
Explicitly connect to first encounter]
    D --> E[Learner internalises second level
Sees what the first level could not show]
    E --> F[Revisit 2: add abstraction or connection
to other concepts in the domain]
    F --> G[Learner can articulate full spiral
From first intuition to formal mastery]
    G --> H[Masterpiece: explains core idea at every level
and why each revisit was necessary]

patterns.example_scenario: A software engineering bootcamp introduces recursion across three modules spaced six weeks apart. In week two, the instructor teaches recursion using a single concrete example: a function that counts down from ten, printing each number, and stopping at zero. The concept of a base case is named but not formalised. In week eight, the class revisits recursion while learning tree traversal — now the instructor names the call stack, introduces the concept of mutual recursion, and shows why iteration is insufficient for hierarchical structures. The prior countdown example is explicitly recalled and contrasted. In week fourteen, recursion appears again in dynamic programming. The instructor introduces memoisation and shows how recursive overlapping sub-problems are the source of exponential blowup. The same recursive structure that was intuitive in week two now becomes the key to polynomial-time solutions. At each revisit, learners who engaged with the prior encounter are visibly faster — the spiral accelerates rather than re-teaches. By the final session, each learner can trace the three encounters and say what each added that the previous one could not have shown.
